kept.text:
A 24-year-old got fired from OpenAI two years ago. He started a hedge fund. In the first half of 2025 it returned 47% while the S&P 500 made just 6%. He owns no Nvidia, no Google, no Amazon. His biggest stock is a fuel cell company.

His name is Leopold Aschenbrenner. German kid, both parents are doctors, finished Columbia at 19 at the top of his class. He worked on AI safety at OpenAI for about a year before they fired him in 2024. He says it was for flagging security problems. That same June he published a 165-page essay predicting that by 2027, AI would be smart enough to do any human’s job. A few months later, the Collison brothers from Stripe and a couple of well-known tech investors gave him money to start a fund.

A year ago the fund held $255 million in stocks. The latest filing shows $5.5 billion. It sits across just 29 companies, with the top ten making up 86% of everything he owns.

His biggest stock is Bloom Energy. They make fuel cells: container-sized power plants you can park right next to a data center so you don’t have to wait years for the local grid to hook you up. The stock is up roughly 1,500% since the start of 2024. Bloom reported earnings on Tuesday: sales more than doubled year-on-year, and the stock jumped 22% the next day.

The rest of the top five is the same idea. Lumentum makes the optical cables AI chips use to talk to each other inside data centers. CoreWeave rents out the AI chips themselves. Core Scientific and IREN are former Bitcoin miners turning their warehouses into AI computing centers.

His whole bet is on the unsexy parts of the AI buildout: power generators, cooling systems, wiring, fiber. He thinks the thing holding AI back is electricity, so he owns the companies selling the shovels.

There’s a catch. A separate filing from early 2025 shows his investors have only put in $383 million. That means the $5.5 billion stock pile is mostly borrowed money, roughly $14 of stocks for every $1 of real cash. When prices climb, the math works beautifully. When they fall, it can wipe out the fund fast.

His one public bet against a stock is on Infosys, the giant Indian IT outsourcing company. The bet: AI coding tools like Claude and ChatGPT will take over what Infosys gets paid to do.

Warren Buffett does the opposite. Berkshire Hathaway, his company, ended last year sitting on $373 billion in cash, which is the largest corporate cash pile in American history. He’s hoarding because nothing looks cheap enough to buy. Aschenbrenner’s top five trade at fourteen times what they sell in a year, and Buffett wouldn’t touch a single one of them.

Whether handing this kid $25 billion is genius or insane comes down to one date. Whether the AI he’s predicting actually arrives close to 2027.

kept.text:
$CRWV CoreWeave price target raised to $150 from $140 at Oppenheimer    

Oppenheimer raised the firm's price target on CoreWeave to $150 from $140 and keeps an Outperform rating on the shares ahead of quarterly results. The firm expects revenue at the high end of guidance and for management to raise 2026 revenue materially above $12B -$13B. This is based on its industry checks that indicate CoreWeave is building out capacity faster than anticipated and can add another 1GW by the September quarter. Oppenheimer also expects the industry-wide shortage of AI data center capacity to persist for multiple years and anticipate that CoreWeave can grow to at least 6GW during that time.

profile_bio.description: Large Model Systems Organization: Join our Slack: https://t.co/vzYOTP4w6C. We developed SGLang https://t.co/OjwQadINKU, Chatbot Arena (now @arena), and Vicuna!

quoted_tweet.text: GB300 NVL72 Rack Scale Dynamo SGLang disaggregation has up to 6.5x better performance than B200 on DeepSeekv4 Pro 1.6T 🚀   The high throughput configuration uses @deepseek_ai 's MegaMoe kernels  which fully fuses & overlaps EP dispatch & EP combine & the GEMMs into an single kernel. Big Shoutout to @CoreWeave to contributing temporary GB300 NVL72 racks towards the open source performance optimization for all to benefit!

kept.text:
12 hours later.

SoftBank -9%. Worst day since November.
CoreWeave -5.8%.
Oracle -4%.
Nvidia -1.6%.

Microsoft +1%. Apple +1.2%.

The market sorted AI today.

The companies who own AI.

The companies who rent OpenAI.

profile_bio.description:
AI is eating everything you were told to build. 

It ate mine first. Now I build things it can't.

quoted_tweet.text:
We all thought OpenAI was flying.

Sam on stage everywhere.

Trillion-dollar valuation talk.

IPO by year-end.

A few annoying lawsuits, sure.

Some competition from Anthropic and Google, fine.

But still the winner. Still the kingmaker.

Then the curtain moved this week.

ChatGPT didn't hit 1 billion users like they promised.

They lost the coding market to Anthropic.

Their CFO is freaking out about $600B in compute bills.

The board is questioning Sam's spending.

The CFO doesn't even want to IPO this year.

Sam's pushing it anyway.

That 'freedom' deal with Microsoft yesterday?

That was Sam running to AWS, Google, and Oracle.

Because one Microsoft couldn't save him.

The whole time we were watching the show.

Nobody was watching the books.

profile_bio.description:
AI is eating everything you were told to build. 

It ate mine first. Now I build things it can't.

tweet.text:
Earnings yesterday made one thing very clear.

There isn’t enough compute available for everything that’s coming.

$CRWV $NBIS $ORCL $IREN and the rest of the neoclouds hold very important keys to the AI future.

Without more compute you don’t get the exponential leap in intelligence, the entire AI trade is bottlenecked by capacity.

Everyone wants more intelligence.

The companies that deliver the compute are becoming some of the most important gatekeepers in the world.

I don’t believe the compute trade is done.

tweet.text:
$APLD is worth watching...

Same AI infrastructure thesis as $IREN and $CIFR... just larger contracts and a longer runway.

> ~$23B in total contracted revenue backlog... at a ~$9.5B market cap. Gap could close rapidly.

> Three signed hyperscaler leases - $CRWV at Polaris Forge 1 ($11B), a second investment-grade hyperscaler at Polaris Forge 2 ($5B), and a third just announced last week at Delta Forge 1 ($7.5B).

> Roth Capital just raised their target to $65.

But, all this is execution dependent.

Want to point out that their financing approach is pretty solid (borrowing against future hyperscaler rent rather than diluting shareholders).

Moving toward a Data Center REIT structure by end of the decade too, which is quite interesting.

Currently sitting at $32 with double Pink Diamonds. Personally waiting for BDs before entering.

tweet.text:
This week’s earnings reports made the infrastructure story hard to ignore.

Every major cloud player is committing billions in capex — not on models, but on the physical layer running them. 

GPUs, data centers, power contracts.
The companies supplying that capacity - $CRWV, $NBIS, $CIFR, $IREN - are called neoclouds. They rent compute to labs & enterprises who can’t build their own infrastructure.

Demand for compute hours is outpacing supply. Labs are on waitlists. Deals are being signed years out.

The bottleneck in AI isn’t intelligence. It’s infrastructure.
